lines: Wilt thou be gone? Sweet Valentine, adieu.
Think on thy Proteus when thou haply seest
Some rare noteworthy object in thy travel.
Wish me partaker in thy happiness
When thou dost meet good hap; and in thy danger,
If ever danger do environ thee,
Commend thy grievance to my holy prayers,
For I will be thy beadsman, Valentine.
fullText: Wilt thou be gone? Sweet Valentine, adieu. Think on thy Proteus when thou haply seest Some rare noteworthy object in thy travel. Wish me partaker in thy happiness When thou dost meet good hap; and in thy danger, If ever danger do environ thee, Commend thy grievance to my holy prayers, For I will be thy beadsman, Valentine.

lines: Upon some book I love I'll pray for thee.
fullText: Upon some book I love I'll pray for thee.

lines: That's a deep story of a deeper love,
For he was more than over shoes in love.
fullText: That's a deep story of a deeper love, For he was more than over shoes in love.

lines: Over the boots? Nay, give me not the boots.
fullText: Over the boots? Nay, give me not the boots.

lines: What?
fullText: What?

lines: So, by your circumstance, you call me fool.
fullText: So, by your circumstance, you call me fool.

lines: 'Tis love you cavil at; I am not Love.
fullText: 'Tis love you cavil at; I am not Love.

lines: Yet writers say: as in the sweetest bud
The eating canker dwells, so eating love
Inhabits in the finest wits of all.
fullText: Yet writers say: as in the sweetest bud The eating canker dwells, so eating love Inhabits in the finest wits of all.

lines: And thither will I bring thee, Valentine.
fullText: And thither will I bring thee, Valentine.

lines: All happiness bechance to thee in Milan.
fullText: All happiness bechance to thee in Milan.

lines: He after honor hunts, I after love.
He leaves his friends, to dignify them more;
I leave myself, my friends, and all, for love.
Thou, Julia, thou hast metamorphosed me,
Made me neglect my studies, lose my time,
War with good counsel, set the world at nought;
Made wit with musing weak, heart sick with thought.
fullText: He after honor hunts, I after love. He leaves his friends, to dignify them more; I leave myself, my friends, and all, for love. Thou, Julia, thou hast metamorphosed me, Made me neglect my studies, lose my time, War with good counsel, set the world at nought; Made wit with musing weak, heart sick with thought.

lines: But now he parted hence to embark for Milan.
fullText: But now he parted hence to embark for Milan.

lines: Indeed a sheep doth very often stray,
An if the shepherd be awhile away.
fullText: Indeed a sheep doth very often stray, An if the shepherd be awhile away.

lines: I do.
fullText: I do.

lines: A silly answer, and fitting well a sheep.
fullText: A silly answer, and fitting well a sheep.

lines: True, and thy master a shepherd.
fullText: True, and thy master a shepherd.

lines: It shall go hard but I'll prove it by another.
fullText: It shall go hard but I'll prove it by another.

lines: The sheep for fodder follow the shepherd; the
shepherd for food follows not the sheep. Thou for
wages followest thy master; thy master for wages
follows not thee. Therefore thou art a sheep.
fullText: The sheep for fodder follow the shepherd; the shepherd for food follows not the sheep. Thou for wages followest thy master; thy master for wages follows not thee. Therefore thou art a sheep.

lines: But dost thou hear? Gav'st thou my letter to
Julia?
fullText: But dost thou hear? Gav'st thou my letter to Julia?

lines: Here's too small a pasture for such store of
muttons.
fullText: Here's too small a pasture for such store of muttons.

lines: Nay, in that you are astray; 'twere best pound
you.
fullText: Nay, in that you are astray; 'twere best pound you.

lines: You mistake; I mean the pound, a pinfold.
fullText: You mistake; I mean the pound, a pinfold.

lines: But what said she?
fullText: But what said she?

lines: Nod--"Ay." Why, that's "noddy."
fullText: Nod--"Ay." Why, that's "noddy."

lines: And that set together is "noddy."
fullText: And that set together is "noddy."

lines: No, no, you shall have it for bearing the letter.
fullText: No, no, you shall have it for bearing the letter.

lines: Why, sir, how do you bear with me?
fullText: Why, sir, how do you bear with me?

lines: Beshrew me, but you have a quick wit.
fullText: Beshrew me, but you have a quick wit.

lines: Come, come, open the matter in brief. What
said she?
fullText: Come, come, open the matter in brief. What said she?

lines: Well, sir, here is for your
pains. What said she?
fullText: Well, sir, here is for your pains. What said she?

lines: Why? Couldst thou perceive so much from
her?
fullText: Why? Couldst thou perceive so much from her?

lines: What said she? Nothing?
fullText: What said she? Nothing?

lines: Go, go, begone, to save your ship from wrack,
Which cannot perish having thee aboard,
Being destined to a drier death on shore.
I must go send some better messenger.
I fear my Julia would not deign my lines,
Receiving them from such a worthless post.
fullText: Go, go, begone, to save your ship from wrack, Which cannot perish having thee aboard, Being destined to a drier death on shore. I must go send some better messenger. I fear my Julia would not deign my lines, Receiving them from such a worthless post.

lines: Sweet love, sweet lines, sweet life!
Here is her hand, the agent of her heart;
Here is her oath for love, her honor's pawn.
O, that our fathers would applaud our loves
To seal our happiness with their consents.
O heavenly Julia!
fullText: Sweet love, sweet lines, sweet life! Here is her hand, the agent of her heart; Here is her oath for love, her honor's pawn. O, that our fathers would applaud our loves To seal our happiness with their consents. O heavenly Julia!

lines: May 't please your Lordship, 'tis a word or two
Of commendations sent from Valentine,
Delivered by a friend that came from him.
fullText: May 't please your Lordship, 'tis a word or two Of commendations sent from Valentine, Delivered by a friend that came from him.

lines: There is no news, my lord, but that he writes
How happily he lives, how well beloved
And daily graced by the Emperor,
Wishing me with him, partner of his fortune.
fullText: There is no news, my lord, but that he writes How happily he lives, how well beloved And daily graced by the Emperor, Wishing me with him, partner of his fortune.

lines: As one relying on your Lordship's will,
And not depending on his friendly wish.
fullText: As one relying on your Lordship's will, And not depending on his friendly wish.

lines: My lord, I cannot be so soon provided.
Please you deliberate a day or two.
fullText: My lord, I cannot be so soon provided. Please you deliberate a day or two.

lines: Thus have I shunned the fire for fear of burning
And drenched me in the sea, where I am drowned.
I feared to show my father Julia's letter
Lest he should take exceptions to my love,
And with the vantage of mine own excuse
Hath he excepted most against my love.
O, how this spring of love resembleth
The uncertain glory of an April day,
Which now shows all the beauty of the sun,
And by and by a cloud takes all away.
fullText: Thus have I shunned the fire for fear of burning And drenched me in the sea, where I am drowned. I feared to show my father Julia's letter Lest he should take exceptions to my love, And with the vantage of mine own excuse Hath he excepted most against my love. O, how this spring of love resembleth The uncertain glory of an April day, Which now shows all the beauty of the sun, And by and by a cloud takes all away.

lines: Why, this it is: my heart accords thereto.
fullText: Why, this it is: my heart accords thereto.

lines: Have patience, gentle Julia.
fullText: Have patience, gentle Julia.

lines: When possibly I can, I will return.
fullText: When possibly I can, I will return.

lines: Why, then we'll make exchange. Here, take you this.
fullText: Why, then we'll make exchange. Here, take you this.

lines: Here is my hand for my true constancy.
And when that hour o'erslips me in the day
Wherein I sigh not, Julia, for thy sake,
The next ensuing hour some foul mischance
Torment me for my love's forgetfulness.
My father stays my coming. Answer not.
The tide is now--nay, not thy tide of tears;
That tide will stay me longer than I should.
Julia, farewell.
What, gone without a word?
Ay, so true love should do. It cannot speak,
For truth hath better deeds than words to grace it.
fullText: Here is my hand for my true constancy. And when that hour o'erslips me in the day Wherein I sigh not, Julia, for thy sake, The next ensuing hour some foul mischance Torment me for my love's forgetfulness. My father stays my coming. Answer not. The tide is now--nay, not thy tide of tears; That tide will stay me longer than I should. Julia, farewell. What, gone without a word? Ay, so true love should do. It cannot speak, For truth hath better deeds than words to grace it.

lines: Go. I come, I come.
fullText: Go. I come, I come.

lines: Not so, sweet lady, but too mean a servant
To have a look of such a worthy mistress.
fullText: Not so, sweet lady, but too mean a servant To have a look of such a worthy mistress.

lines: My duty will I boast of, nothing else.
fullText: My duty will I boast of, nothing else.

lines: I'll die on him that says so but yourself.
fullText: I'll die on him that says so but yourself.

lines: That you are worthless.
fullText: That you are worthless.

lines: We'll both attend upon your Ladyship.
fullText: We'll both attend upon your Ladyship.

lines: Your friends are well and have them much
commended.
fullText: Your friends are well and have them much commended.

lines: I left them all in health.
fullText: I left them all in health.

lines: My tales of love were wont to weary you.
I know you joy not in a love discourse.
fullText: My tales of love were wont to weary you. I know you joy not in a love discourse.

lines: Enough; I read your fortune in your eye.
Was this the idol that you worship so?
fullText: Enough; I read your fortune in your eye. Was this the idol that you worship so?

lines: No, but she is an earthly paragon.
fullText: No, but she is an earthly paragon.

lines: I will not flatter her.
fullText: I will not flatter her.

lines: When I was sick, you gave me bitter pills,
And I must minister the like to you.
fullText: When I was sick, you gave me bitter pills, And I must minister the like to you.

lines: Except my mistress.
fullText: Except my mistress.

lines: Have I not reason to prefer mine own?
fullText: Have I not reason to prefer mine own?

lines: Why, Valentine, what braggartism is this?
fullText: Why, Valentine, what braggartism is this?

lines: Then let her alone.
fullText: Then let her alone.

lines: But she loves you?
fullText: But she loves you?

lines: Go on before. I shall inquire you forth.
I must unto the road to disembark
Some necessaries that I needs must use,
And then I'll presently attend you.
fullText: Go on before. I shall inquire you forth. I must unto the road to disembark Some necessaries that I needs must use, And then I'll presently attend you.

lines: I will.
Even as one heat another heat expels,
Or as one nail by strength drives out another,
So the remembrance of my former love
Is by a newer object quite forgotten.
Is it mine eye, or Valentine's praise,
Her true perfection, or my false transgression,
That makes me reasonless to reason thus?
She is fair, and so is Julia that I love--
That I did love, for now my love is thawed,
Which like a waxen image 'gainst a fire
Bears no impression of the thing it was.
Methinks my zeal to Valentine is cold,
And that I love him not as I was wont.
O, but I love his lady too too much,
And that's the reason I love him so little.
How shall I dote on her with more advice
That thus without advice begin to love her?
'Tis but her picture I have yet beheld,
And that hath dazzled my reason's light;
But when I look on her perfections,
There is no reason but I shall be blind.
If I can check my erring love, I will;
If not, to compass her I'll use my skill.
fullText: I will. Even as one heat another heat expels, Or as one nail by strength drives out another, So the remembrance of my former love Is by a newer object quite forgotten. Is it mine eye, or Valentine's praise, Her true perfection, or my false transgression, That makes me reasonless to reason thus? She is fair, and so is Julia that I love-- That I did love, for now my love is thawed, Which like a waxen image 'gainst a fire Bears no impression of the thing it was. Methinks my zeal to Valentine is cold, And that I love him not as I was wont. O, but I love his lady too too much, And that's the reason I love him so little. How shall I dote on her with more advice That thus without advice begin to love her? 'Tis but her picture I have yet beheld, And that hath dazzled my reason's light; But when I look on her perfections, There is no reason but I shall be blind. If I can check my erring love, I will; If not, to compass her I'll use my skill.

lines: To leave my Julia, shall I be forsworn.
To love fair Sylvia, shall I be forsworn.
To wrong my friend, I shall be much forsworn.
And ev'n that power which gave me first my oath
Provokes me to this threefold perjury.
Love bade me swear, and love bids me forswear.
O sweet-suggesting Love, if thou hast sinned,
Teach me, thy tempted subject, to excuse it.
At first I did adore a twinkling star,
But now I worship a celestial sun;
Unheedful vows may heedfully be broken,
And he wants wit that wants resolved will
To learn his wit t' exchange the bad for better.
Fie, fie, unreverend tongue, to call her bad
Whose sovereignty so oft thou hast preferred
With twenty thousand soul-confirming oaths.
I cannot leave to love, and yet I do.
But there I leave to love where I should love.
Julia I lose, and Valentine I lose;
If I keep them, I needs must lose myself;
If I lose them, thus find I by their loss:
For Valentine, myself; for Julia, Sylvia.
I to myself am dearer than a friend,
For love is still most precious in itself,
And Sylvia--witness heaven that made her fair--
Shows Julia but a swarthy Ethiope.
I will forget that Julia is alive,
Rememb'ring that my love to her is dead;
And Valentine I'll hold an enemy,
Aiming at Sylvia as a sweeter friend.
I cannot now prove constant to myself
Without some treachery used to Valentine.
This night he meaneth with a corded ladder
To climb celestial Sylvia's chamber window,
Myself in counsel his competitor.
Now presently I'll give her father notice
Of their disguising and pretended flight,
Who, all enraged, will banish Valentine,
For Thurio he intends shall wed his daughter.
But Valentine being gone, I'll quickly cross
By some sly trick blunt Thurio's dull proceeding.
Love, lend me wings to make my purpose swift,
As thou hast lent me wit to plot this drift.
fullText: To leave my Julia, shall I be forsworn. To love fair Sylvia, shall I be forsworn. To wrong my friend, I shall be much forsworn. And ev'n that power which gave me first my oath Provokes me to this threefold perjury. Love bade me swear, and love bids me forswear. O sweet-suggesting Love, if thou hast sinned, Teach me, thy tempted subject, to excuse it. At first I did adore a twinkling star, But now I worship a celestial sun; Unheedful vows may heedfully be broken, And he wants wit that wants resolved will To learn his wit t' exchange the bad for better. Fie, fie, unreverend tongue, to call her bad Whose sovereignty so oft thou hast preferred With twenty thousand soul-confirming oaths. I cannot leave to love, and yet I do. But there I leave to love where I should love. Julia I lose, and Valentine I lose; If I keep them, I needs must lose myself; If I lose them, thus find I by their loss: For Valentine, myself; for Julia, Sylvia. I to myself am dearer than a friend, For love is still most precious in itself, And Sylvia--witness heaven that made her fair-- Shows Julia but a swarthy Ethiope. I will forget that Julia is alive, Rememb'ring that my love to her is dead; And Valentine I'll hold an enemy, Aiming at Sylvia as a sweeter friend. I cannot now prove constant to myself Without some treachery used to Valentine. This night he meaneth with a corded ladder To climb celestial Sylvia's chamber window, Myself in counsel his competitor. Now presently I'll give her father notice Of their disguising and pretended flight, Who, all enraged, will banish Valentine, For Thurio he intends shall wed his daughter. But Valentine being gone, I'll quickly cross By some sly trick blunt Thurio's dull proceeding. Love, lend me wings to make my purpose swift, As thou hast lent me wit to plot this drift.

lines: My gracious lord, that which I would discover
The law of friendship bids me to conceal,
But when I call to mind your gracious favors
Done to me, undeserving as I am,
My duty pricks me on to utter that
Which else no worldly good should draw from me.
Know, worthy prince, Sir Valentine my friend
This night intends to steal away your daughter;
Myself am one made privy to the plot.
I know you have determined to bestow her
On Thurio, whom your gentle daughter hates,
And should she thus be stol'n away from you,
It would be much vexation to your age.
Thus, for my duty's sake, I rather chose
To cross my friend in his intended drift
Than, by concealing it, heap on your head
A pack of sorrows which would press you down,
Being unprevented, to your timeless grave.
fullText: My gracious lord, that which I would discover The law of friendship bids me to conceal, But when I call to mind your gracious favors Done to me, undeserving as I am, My duty pricks me on to utter that Which else no worldly good should draw from me. Know, worthy prince, Sir Valentine my friend This night intends to steal away your daughter; Myself am one made privy to the plot. I know you have determined to bestow her On Thurio, whom your gentle daughter hates, And should she thus be stol'n away from you, It would be much vexation to your age. Thus, for my duty's sake, I rather chose To cross my friend in his intended drift Than, by concealing it, heap on your head A pack of sorrows which would press you down, Being unprevented, to your timeless grave.

lines: Know, noble lord, they have devised a mean
How he her chamber-window will ascend
And with a corded ladder fetch her down;
For which the youthful lover now is gone,
And this way comes he with it presently,
Where, if it please you, you may intercept him.
But, good my lord, do it so cunningly
That my discovery be not aimed at;
For love of you, not hate unto my friend,
Hath made me publisher of this pretense.
fullText: Know, noble lord, they have devised a mean How he her chamber-window will ascend And with a corded ladder fetch her down; For which the youthful lover now is gone, And this way comes he with it presently, Where, if it please you, you may intercept him. But, good my lord, do it so cunningly That my discovery be not aimed at; For love of you, not hate unto my friend, Hath made me publisher of this pretense.

lines: Adieu, my lord. Sir Valentine is coming.
fullText: Adieu, my lord. Sir Valentine is coming.

lines: Run, boy, run, run, and seek him out.
fullText: Run, boy, run, run, and seek him out.

lines: What seest thou?
fullText: What seest thou?

lines: Valentine?
fullText: Valentine?

lines: Who then? His spirit?
fullText: Who then? His spirit?

lines: What then?
fullText: What then?

lines: Who wouldst thou strike?
fullText: Who wouldst thou strike?

lines: Villain, forbear.
fullText: Villain, forbear.

lines: Sirrah, I say forbear.--Friend Valentine, a word.
fullText: Sirrah, I say forbear.--Friend Valentine, a word.

lines: Then in dumb silence will I bury mine,
For they are harsh, untunable, and bad.
fullText: Then in dumb silence will I bury mine, For they are harsh, untunable, and bad.

lines: No, Valentine.
fullText: No, Valentine.

lines: No, Valentine.
fullText: No, Valentine.

lines: That thou art banished--O, that's the news--
From hence, from Sylvia, and from me thy friend.
fullText: That thou art banished--O, that's the news-- From hence, from Sylvia, and from me thy friend.

lines: Ay, ay, and she hath offered to the doom--
Which unreversed stands in effectual force--
A sea of melting pearl, which some call tears;
Those at her father's churlish feet she tendered,
With them, upon her knees, her humble self,
Wringing her hands, whose whiteness so became
them
As if but now they waxed pale for woe.
But neither bended knees, pure hands held up,
Sad sighs, deep groans, nor silver-shedding tears
Could penetrate her uncompassionate sire;
But Valentine, if he be ta'en, must die.
Besides, her intercession chafed him so,
When she for thy repeal was suppliant,
That to close prison he commanded her
With many bitter threats of biding there.
fullText: Ay, ay, and she hath offered to the doom-- Which unreversed stands in effectual force-- A sea of melting pearl, which some call tears; Those at her father's churlish feet she tendered, With them, upon her knees, her humble self, Wringing her hands, whose whiteness so became them As if but now they waxed pale for woe. But neither bended knees, pure hands held up, Sad sighs, deep groans, nor silver-shedding tears Could penetrate her uncompassionate sire; But Valentine, if he be ta'en, must die. Besides, her intercession chafed him so, When she for thy repeal was suppliant, That to close prison he commanded her With many bitter threats of biding there.

lines: Cease to lament for that thou canst not help,
And study help for that which thou lament'st.
Time is the nurse and breeder of all good.
Here, if thou stay, thou canst not see thy love;
Besides, thy staying will abridge thy life.
Hope is a lover's staff; walk hence with that
And manage it against despairing thoughts.
Thy letters may be here, though thou art hence,
Which, being writ to me, shall be delivered
Even in the milk-white bosom of thy love.
The time now serves not to expostulate.
Come, I'll convey thee through the city gate
And, ere I part with thee, confer at large
Of all that may concern thy love affairs.
As thou lov'st Sylvia, though not for thyself,
Regard thy danger, and along with me.
fullText: Cease to lament for that thou canst not help, And study help for that which thou lament'st. Time is the nurse and breeder of all good. Here, if thou stay, thou canst not see thy love; Besides, thy staying will abridge thy life. Hope is a lover's staff; walk hence with that And manage it against despairing thoughts. Thy letters may be here, though thou art hence, Which, being writ to me, shall be delivered Even in the milk-white bosom of thy love. The time now serves not to expostulate. Come, I'll convey thee through the city gate And, ere I part with thee, confer at large Of all that may concern thy love affairs. As thou lov'st Sylvia, though not for thyself, Regard thy danger, and along with me.

lines: Go, sirrah, find him out.--Come, Valentine.
fullText: Go, sirrah, find him out.--Come, Valentine.

lines: Gone, my good lord.
fullText: Gone, my good lord.

lines: A little time, my lord, will kill that grief.
fullText: A little time, my lord, will kill that grief.

lines: Longer than I prove loyal to your Grace
Let me not live to look upon your Grace.
fullText: Longer than I prove loyal to your Grace Let me not live to look upon your Grace.

lines: I do, my lord.
fullText: I do, my lord.

lines: She did, my lord, when Valentine was here.
fullText: She did, my lord, when Valentine was here.

lines: The best way is to slander Valentine
With falsehood, cowardice, and poor descent,
Three things that women highly hold in hate.
fullText: The best way is to slander Valentine With falsehood, cowardice, and poor descent, Three things that women highly hold in hate.

lines: Ay, if his enemy deliver it.
Therefore it must with circumstance be spoken
By one whom she esteemeth as his friend.
fullText: Ay, if his enemy deliver it. Therefore it must with circumstance be spoken By one whom she esteemeth as his friend.

lines: And that, my lord, I shall be loath to do.
'Tis an ill office for a gentleman,
Especially against his very friend.
fullText: And that, my lord, I shall be loath to do. 'Tis an ill office for a gentleman, Especially against his very friend.

lines: You have prevailed, my lord. If I can do it
By aught that I can speak in his dispraise,
She shall not long continue love to him.
But say this weed her love from Valentine,
It follows not that she will love Sir Thurio.
fullText: You have prevailed, my lord. If I can do it By aught that I can speak in his dispraise, She shall not long continue love to him. But say this weed her love from Valentine, It follows not that she will love Sir Thurio.

lines: As much as I can do I will effect.--
But you, Sir Thurio, are not sharp enough.
You must lay lime to tangle her desires
By wailful sonnets, whose composed rhymes
Should be full-fraught with serviceable vows.
fullText: As much as I can do I will effect.-- But you, Sir Thurio, are not sharp enough. You must lay lime to tangle her desires By wailful sonnets, whose composed rhymes Should be full-fraught with serviceable vows.

lines: Say that upon the altar of her beauty
You sacrifice your tears, your sighs, your heart.
Write till your ink be dry, and with your tears
Moist it again, and frame some feeling line
That may discover such integrity.
For Orpheus' lute was strung with poets' sinews,
Whose golden touch could soften steel and stones,
Make tigers tame, and huge leviathans
Forsake unsounded deeps to dance on sands.
After your dire-lamenting elegies,
Visit by night your lady's chamber window
With some sweet consort; to their instruments
Tune a deploring dump; the night's dead silence
Will well become such sweet complaining
grievance.
This, or else nothing, will inherit her.
fullText: Say that upon the altar of her beauty You sacrifice your tears, your sighs, your heart. Write till your ink be dry, and with your tears Moist it again, and frame some feeling line That may discover such integrity. For Orpheus' lute was strung with poets' sinews, Whose golden touch could soften steel and stones, Make tigers tame, and huge leviathans Forsake unsounded deeps to dance on sands. After your dire-lamenting elegies, Visit by night your lady's chamber window With some sweet consort; to their instruments Tune a deploring dump; the night's dead silence Will well become such sweet complaining grievance. This, or else nothing, will inherit her.

lines: We'll wait upon your Grace till after supper
And afterward determine our proceedings.
fullText: We'll wait upon your Grace till after supper And afterward determine our proceedings.

lines: Already have I been false to Valentine,
And now I must be as unjust to Thurio.
Under the color of commending him,
I have access my own love to prefer.
But Sylvia is too fair, too true, too holy
To be corrupted with my worthless gifts.
When I protest true loyalty to her,
She twits me with my falsehood to my friend;
When to her beauty I commend my vows,
She bids me think how I have been forsworn
In breaking faith with Julia, whom I loved;
And notwithstanding all her sudden quips,
The least whereof would quell a lover's hope,
Yet, spaniel-like, the more she spurns my love,
The more it grows and fawneth on her still.
But here comes Thurio. Now must we to her
window
And give some evening music to her ear.
fullText: Already have I been false to Valentine, And now I must be as unjust to Thurio. Under the color of commending him, I have access my own love to prefer. But Sylvia is too fair, too true, too holy To be corrupted with my worthless gifts. When I protest true loyalty to her, She twits me with my falsehood to my friend; When to her beauty I commend my vows, She bids me think how I have been forsworn In breaking faith with Julia, whom I loved; And notwithstanding all her sudden quips, The least whereof would quell a lover's hope, Yet, spaniel-like, the more she spurns my love, The more it grows and fawneth on her still. But here comes Thurio. Now must we to her window And give some evening music to her ear.

lines: Ay, gentle Thurio, for you know that love
Will creep in service where it cannot go.
fullText: Ay, gentle Thurio, for you know that love Will creep in service where it cannot go.

lines: Sir, but I do, or else I would be hence.
fullText: Sir, but I do, or else I would be hence.

lines: Ay, Sylvia, for your sake.
fullText: Ay, Sylvia, for your sake.

lines: Sir Thurio, fear not you. I will so plead
That you shall say my cunning drift excels.
fullText: Sir Thurio, fear not you. I will so plead That you shall say my cunning drift excels.

lines: At Saint Gregory's well.
fullText: At Saint Gregory's well.

lines: Madam, good even to your Ladyship.
fullText: Madam, good even to your Ladyship.

lines: One, lady, if you knew his pure heart's truth,
You would quickly learn to know him by his voice.
fullText: One, lady, if you knew his pure heart's truth, You would quickly learn to know him by his voice.

lines: Sir Proteus, gentle lady, and your servant.
fullText: Sir Proteus, gentle lady, and your servant.

lines: That I may compass yours.
fullText: That I may compass yours.

lines: I grant, sweet love, that I did love a lady,
But she is dead.
fullText: I grant, sweet love, that I did love a lady, But she is dead.

lines: I likewise hear that Valentine is dead.
fullText: I likewise hear that Valentine is dead.

lines: Sweet lady, let me rake it from the earth.
fullText: Sweet lady, let me rake it from the earth.

lines: Madam, if your heart be so obdurate,
Vouchsafe me yet your picture for my love,
The picture that is hanging in your chamber;
To that I'll speak, to that I'll sigh and weep,
For since the substance of your perfect self
Is else devoted, I am but a shadow;
And to your shadow will I make true love.
fullText: Madam, if your heart be so obdurate, Vouchsafe me yet your picture for my love, The picture that is hanging in your chamber; To that I'll speak, to that I'll sigh and weep, For since the substance of your perfect self Is else devoted, I am but a shadow; And to your shadow will I make true love.

lines: As wretches have o'ernight
That wait for execution in the morn.
fullText: As wretches have o'ernight That wait for execution in the morn.

lines: Sebastian is thy name? I like thee well
And will employ thee in some service presently.
fullText: Sebastian is thy name? I like thee well And will employ thee in some service presently.

lines: I hope thou wilt. How now, you
whoreson peasant?
Where have you been these two days loitering?
fullText: I hope thou wilt. How now, you whoreson peasant? Where have you been these two days loitering?

lines: And what says she to my little jewel?
fullText: And what says she to my little jewel?

lines: But she received my dog?
fullText: But she received my dog?

lines: What, didst thou offer her this from me?
fullText: What, didst thou offer her this from me?

lines: Go, get thee hence, and find my dog again,
Or ne'er return again into my sight.
Away, I say. Stayest thou to vex me here?
A slave that still an end turns me to shame.
Sebastian, I have entertained thee,
Partly that I have need of such a youth
That can with some discretion do my business--
For 'tis no trusting to yond foolish lout--
But chiefly for thy face and thy behavior,
Which, if my augury deceive me not,
Witness good bringing-up, fortune, and truth.
Therefore, know thou, for this I entertain thee.
Go presently, and take this ring with thee;
Deliver it to Madam Sylvia.
She loved me well delivered it to me.
fullText: Go, get thee hence, and find my dog again, Or ne'er return again into my sight. Away, I say. Stayest thou to vex me here? A slave that still an end turns me to shame. Sebastian, I have entertained thee, Partly that I have need of such a youth That can with some discretion do my business-- For 'tis no trusting to yond foolish lout-- But chiefly for thy face and thy behavior, Which, if my augury deceive me not, Witness good bringing-up, fortune, and truth. Therefore, know thou, for this I entertain thee. Go presently, and take this ring with thee; Deliver it to Madam Sylvia. She loved me well delivered it to me.

lines: Not so; I think she lives.
fullText: Not so; I think she lives.

lines: Why dost thou cry "Alas"?
fullText: Why dost thou cry "Alas"?

lines: Wherefore shouldst thou pity her?
fullText: Wherefore shouldst thou pity her?

lines: Well, give her that ring and therewithal
This letter. That's her
chamber. Tell my lady
I claim the promise for her heavenly picture.
Your message done, hie home unto my chamber,
Where thou shalt find me sad and solitary.
fullText: Well, give her that ring and therewithal This letter. That's her chamber. Tell my lady I claim the promise for her heavenly picture. Your message done, hie home unto my chamber, Where thou shalt find me sad and solitary.

lines: O sir, I find her milder than she was,
And yet she takes exceptions at your person.
fullText: O sir, I find her milder than she was, And yet she takes exceptions at your person.

lines: No, that it is too little.
fullText: No, that it is too little.

lines: She says it is a fair one.
fullText: She says it is a fair one.

lines: But pearls are fair, and the old saying is,
Black men are pearls in beauteous ladies' eyes.
fullText: But pearls are fair, and the old saying is, Black men are pearls in beauteous ladies' eyes.

lines: Ill, when you talk of war.
fullText: Ill, when you talk of war.

lines: O, sir, she makes no doubt of that.
fullText: O, sir, she makes no doubt of that.

lines: That you are well derived.
fullText: That you are well derived.

lines: O, ay, and pities them.
fullText: O, ay, and pities them.

lines: That they are out by lease.
fullText: That they are out by lease.

lines: Nor I.
fullText: Nor I.

lines: Neither.
fullText: Neither.

lines: And I will follow, more for Sylvia's love
Than hate of Eglamour that goes with her.
fullText: And I will follow, more for Sylvia's love Than hate of Eglamour that goes with her.

lines: Madam, this service I have done for you--
Though you respect not aught your servant doth--
To hazard life, and rescue you from him
That would have forced your honor and your love.
Vouchsafe me for my meed but one fair look;
A smaller boon than this I cannot beg,
And less than this I am sure you cannot give.
fullText: Madam, this service I have done for you-- Though you respect not aught your servant doth-- To hazard life, and rescue you from him That would have forced your honor and your love. Vouchsafe me for my meed but one fair look; A smaller boon than this I cannot beg, And less than this I am sure you cannot give.

lines: Unhappy were you, madam, ere I came,
But by my coming, I have made you happy.
fullText: Unhappy were you, madam, ere I came, But by my coming, I have made you happy.

lines: What dangerous action, stood it next to death,
Would I not undergo for one calm look!
O, 'tis the curse in love, and still approved,
When women cannot love where they're beloved.
fullText: What dangerous action, stood it next to death, Would I not undergo for one calm look! O, 'tis the curse in love, and still approved, When women cannot love where they're beloved.

lines: In love
Who respects friend?
fullText: In love Who respects friend?

lines: Nay, if the gentle spirit of moving words
Can no way change you to a milder form,
I'll woo you like a soldier, at arms' end,
And love you 'gainst the nature of love--force you.
fullText: Nay, if the gentle spirit of moving words Can no way change you to a milder form, I'll woo you like a soldier, at arms' end, And love you 'gainst the nature of love--force you.

lines: I'll force thee yield to my desire.
fullText: I'll force thee yield to my desire.

lines: Valentine!
fullText: Valentine!

lines: My shame and guilt confounds me.
Forgive me, Valentine. If hearty sorrow
Be a sufficient ransom for offense,
I tender 't here. I do as truly suffer
As e'er I did commit.
fullText: My shame and guilt confounds me. Forgive me, Valentine. If hearty sorrow Be a sufficient ransom for offense, I tender 't here. I do as truly suffer As e'er I did commit.

lines: Look to the boy.
fullText: Look to the boy.

lines: Where is that ring, boy?
fullText: Where is that ring, boy?

lines: How, let me see.
Why, this is the ring I gave to Julia.
fullText: How, let me see. Why, this is the ring I gave to Julia.

lines: But how cam'st thou by this ring? At my depart
I gave this unto Julia.
fullText: But how cam'st thou by this ring? At my depart I gave this unto Julia.

lines: How? Julia!
fullText: How? Julia!

lines: "Than men their minds"? 'Tis true. O heaven, were
man
But constant, he were perfect; that one error
Fills him with faults, makes him run through all th'
sins;
Inconstancy falls off ere it begins.
What is in Sylvia's face but I may spy
More fresh in Julia's, with a constant eye?
fullText: "Than men their minds"? 'Tis true. O heaven, were man But constant, he were perfect; that one error Fills him with faults, makes him run through all th' sins; Inconstancy falls off ere it begins. What is in Sylvia's face but I may spy More fresh in Julia's, with a constant eye?

lines: Bear witness, heaven, I have my wish forever.
fullText: Bear witness, heaven, I have my wish forever.
